text.heading: MODERATION EXERCISE
text.p: A well conceived test item must pass 2 'tests'
a) the test of expert judgement
b) the test of use

The test of use is the pretest/trial test which is essential to provide considerable information for statistical analysis of data. The "Facility" value and "discrimination" index are the most useful of all the statistics obtainable from this analysis. The use of these statistics in the determination of the specific items to be included in a question paper or be rejected is the act of Moderation or Item analysis. This is a quantitative approach to the evaluation of test items.
On the other hand, the test of expert judgement in a more subjective approach- It is the vetting or shredding of the items. This is a qualitative approach to the act of Moderation.
text.heading: Criteria to be used in Item Vetting/Shredding:
text.p: 1. What section of the syllabus does the item test? (Year? topic)
2. What ability/thinking level does the item test? (A/B/C/D/E)
3. Is the language/mathematics/science sound or technically correct? i.e. is the content of the question correct language-wise, etc.
4. Are there clues to the key (examine the length or the clue grammatical inconsistencies, specific determinants or other writing defects),
5. What is the key?
6. Are the detractors plausible?
7. Is there more than one defensible answer?
8. Is it reasonable to expect an average examinee to answer the item in the allotted time.
9. Where appropriate, are the responses in the correct sequences.
10. Can the wording be improved by eliminating unnecessary details, over sophistication, factoring out.

When item vetters have subjected the items to these rigorous examination, the level of acceptability of each item can be determined. If a major amendment is required on an item to enable it meet with the criteria laid down, then that item should be rejected. Where necessary amendments can be made on items that are defective, they should be carried out to make them conform to the prevailing criteria.
Attached herewith is a checklist for vetting and moderating both multiple choice items and essay/practical type items.
- Are options which are names arranged alphabetically?
- Are the options overloaded?
- Are repetitive parts included in the stem?
text.heading: D. The key
text.p: - Does the key answer the questions?
- Does it stand out from the rest?
- Is it the only answer, to the question asked?
- Are there words in the stem that may give out the key?
- Is the key to one item given in another item when both are given in the same test? '
text.heading: E. Distractors
text.p: - Is the distractor plausible or sharp enough?
- Is there any other correct option?
- Does any one of them attract unnecessary attention?
text.heading: 1.3 EDITING ESSAY/PRACTICAL TYPE ITEMS
text.p: The following points should be noted when writing Essay/Practical type items:
text.heading: A. The Question:
text.p: (i) Must be within the syllabus.
(ii) Must be concise, clear and unambiguous.
(iii) Should be structured directly so that the candidates do not have to guess.
(iy) If subdivided, should be arranged in logical order.
(v) The number of points for which full marks will be given should be indicated e.g. "State four advantates of" preferable to "Discuss the advantages of"
(vi) Indicate the type of response expected from candidates e.g. calculate, solve, write, draw.
(vii) The length of the answer expected should be indicated in the wording of the question e.g. "List", "Name", "State", suggest few words or brief answers; while 'describe', indicate longer answer.

'Calculate' wants more accurate answers than "Sketch." Words like "write note on." discuss etc. leave the candidates in doubt as to the length and depth of answer required and should normally be avoided.
(viii) The use of the word 'You' should normally be avoided. However, 'on your diagram label... is acceptable.
(ix) Any necessary information should be given in the question rather than in the rubric as candidates will forget e.g. TT is 3.142.
(x) The use of trade names should be avoided.
(xi) Diagrams, symbols, abbreviations, units and terminology used in questions must conform to the current standards abbreviations and conventions in use.
(xii) Adequate time for the average candidate to write the answer to the question.
(xiii) Each questions must be of the same standard as that of other questions if there is a choice.
(xiv) Each question must be moderately difficult, and should be able to separate the very good candidates from the average and the very poor ones.
(xv) Question should not contain anything to give undue advantage to any religious, tribal or ethnic group.
(xvi) Questions should not be expensive to develop - use diagrams if only necessary to clarify the questions.
text.heading: 1.4 THE SOLUTION/MARKING SCHEME:
text.p: (i) Solutions and marking schemes must accompany all draft questions.
(ii) Numerical questions should be accompanied with full solutions.
(iii) The marks awarded for each part of the question must be indicated.
(iv) Do not award marks for writing ability, neatness or presentation unless they are essential to the subject being assessed.
text.heading: 2.1 MODERATION OF ESSAY ITEMS:
text.p: The Moderating Committee must thoroughly go through each item and the marking scheme to ensure that the:
(i) item is syllabic;
(ii) item is workable and can be completed within the same time given;
(iii) language of the item is within the reach of the majority of the candidates for whom it is meant;
(iv) item is clearly worded and unabiguous;
(v) item is not a verbatim repetition of a past item;
(vi) marking scheme is adequately comprehensive;
(vii) allocation of marks to point is fair to all candidates neither too stingy nor too generous.

Each test item should be amended by the moderators in accordance with i - vii above. Every moderator should reflect all such amendments on his/her copy. Items that are so bad that they cannot be salvaged should be rejected and must be so indicated on every moderator's copy to ensure that they are not selected in error onto the final test paper.
text.heading: 2.2 MODERATION OF OBJECTIVE ITEMS:
text.p: Each objective item moderated must ensure that:
(i) item is within the scope of the syllabus;
(ii) the item is clearly worded and unambiguous;
(iii) there is an agreement of the item with the options in tense, preposition and number;
(iv) there is only one key;
(v) all the distractors are plausible and are derivable from possible common errors on the topic;
(vi) the length of the key does not give it out;
(vii) the key is not too frequently placed in any one position;
(viii) the item is testing only one thing at a time;
(ix) the alphabets of the options (A-E) are not used to label appended diagram;
(x) options involving numbers and figures are arranged in either ascending or descending order;
(xi) the item is workable within the time given;
(xii) the item contains no hidden trick.
text.heading: ITEM EDITING
text.p: Items should be edited by subject officer(s) as soon as the items are received from the examiners. An item editor must be a subject expert as well as be familiar with the techniques of item writing.
Items may be accepted, modified, rejected or referred to the writer for amendment.
An item editor edits items to ensure that:
(i) their structure does not contain any mistake and conforms to the principles outlined below;
(ii) their position in the table of specification is adhered to;
(iii) they test the intended ability - Recall, Comprehension, Application, Analysis, Synthesis, Evaluation.
(iv) there is only one correct option (in multiple choice item).
text.heading: 1.2 EDITING MULTIPLE CHOICE ITEMS
text.p: A multiple choice item comprises a stem and options. The options comprise plausible distractors and the key. The following points should be considered for editing purposes:
A. The Item As a Whole:
- Is the item testing what it is supposed to test?
- Is there a better way of testing what the item tests?
- Is it within the scope of the syllabus?
- Is it worth asking?
- Is it clearly stated or is it ambiguous for the level of candidates?
- Does the item have the test specification number indicated?
- Does the diagram or graph agree with the stem and options?
- Does the item test too many things at once?
- Are there vague expressions?
- Are there any double negatives?
B. The stem:
- Does the stem clearly define the aim of a specific problems?
- Does it contain unneccessary information or does it need more information?
- Can it be better or clearly worded?
- Is it technically correct?
- If unavoidably worded in the negative; is the negative term written in capitals?
- Does the stem start with "which one of the following...?" instead of "which of the following...?"
C. The Options:
- Are the options similar in structure and length? ( Semantecallyand grammatically)
- Do they overlap?
- Do they complete the stem?
- Are numerical ones arranged in order of magnitude?
